title: Grok 4.5's first-day verdict: cheaper, faster, and hallucinating twice as often
subtitle: Independent benchmarks 24 hours after launch put xAI's new model fourth on the intelligence leaderboard, but its hallucination rate jumped from 25% to 54% — the accuracy trade nobody headlined at launch.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Twenty-four hours after xAI released Grok 4.5, the independent benchmarks landed and complicated the launch narrative. Artificial Analysis placed the model fourth on its Intelligence Index at a score of 54, behind Claude Fable 5, GPT-5.5, and Claude Opus 4.8 — a strong result at a fraction of the price, and the best agentic tool-use score in the field. But its hallucination rate on the AA-Omniscience Index jumped from 25% on Grok 4.3 to 54% on Grok 4.5, even as raw factual accuracy improved from 35% to 52%.

The mechanism is the mixture-of-experts architecture xAI used to make the model cheap. Each token is routed to a specialized subnetwork, which increases knowledge breadth at scale but also makes the model more confident about the parts it gets wrong. It knows more, and it sounds more certain about all of it — including the errors. Grok 4.5 running in xAI's Grok Build harness scores 76 on the Coding Agent Index, matching GPT-5.5 in Codex and trailing Fable 5 in Claude Code by one point, at roughly a fifth of the cost.

That cost-quality shape matters more than the ranking. For agentic workloads that fire off hundreds of tool calls per task, a small hallucination on any one call can compound into a broken output — but the price gap opens up new deployment envelopes for use cases where the errors are cheap to detect and cheap to retry. It is also a reminder that headline benchmark scores hide the shape of the failures underneath, which is the number that determines whether a model is safe to put in production.

For learners: never trust a single benchmark number. The Intelligence Index rank tells you nothing about how the model fails — and how a model fails is what determines whether you can build on it. When you evaluate any model, run it on your own domain, measure the hallucination rate specifically, and look at the confidence of the wrong answers. A model that says 'I don't know' when it doesn't know is worth more than one that scores five points higher on a leaderboard and confidently invents citations.
